headline: Scientists Race to Save Ghana's Guitarfish From Extinction
meta_description: Marine biologist Issah Seidu is working with fishing communities to protect guitarfish before they disappear entirely from West African waters.
summary_short: A Ghanaian marine biologist is mobilizing fishing communities to save guitarfish from the same fate as sawfish and wedgefish—species already fished to local extinction.
body:
Marine biologist Issah Seidu watched two elasmobranch species vanish from Ghanaian waters: sawfish disappeared from local catches in the 1980s, and wedgefish haven't been caught in over eight years. Now guitarfish—a group of rays with valuable fins—face the same trajectory toward extinction. This year, Seidu received a Whitley Award to support his NGO, AquaLife, in partnering with fishing communities across Ghana to research and protect guitarfish before they too are lost. The work addresses a crisis driven by overfishing of traditional catches like tuna and mackerel, which has pushed fishers to target increasingly vulnerable species and their lucrative fins for export to Asian markets.

Seidu's 2022 research revealed the scale of the problem. Surveys at landing and market sites showed that more than half the spineback guitarfish caught were not yet sexually mature—a sign populations are being harvested faster than they can reproduce. Fishers themselves reported that catches of two larger guitarfish species had collapsed by up to 90%, while smaller species saw declines of roughly 50%. The animals are hunted so intensively that specialized fishing camps migrate across West Africa, from Ghana to Ivory Coast, Togo, Benin, and beyond, targeting guitarfish with specialized gillnets and selling their prized white fins to traders who distribute them across the region and onward to Asia.

AquaLife's approach tackles the problem from within fishing communities themselves. Working in five communities in Ghana's Ahanta West district, the organization collaborates with both resident and migrant fishers to gather breeding data, raise ecological awareness, and develop alternative livelihoods. Partnering with the Ghana Fishery Commission at multiple levels, AquaLife educates fishers on sustainable practices while advocating for stronger governance—part of a broader push to embed guitarfish conservation into Ghana's marine protected areas and coastal fisheries management.
quick_facts: Issah Seidu founded AquaLife, a marine conservation NGO in Ghana
Sawfish vanished from Ghanaian waters by the 1980s; wedgefish unseen for 8+ years
Guitarfish catches of larger species collapsed by up to 90%, fishers report
AquaLife works with five communities in Ghana's Ahanta West district
Guitarfish fins exported across West Africa and onward to Asian markets
